title: Coroner unsure if boy's death after eating burrito bowl was preventable
text:
A coroner cannot determine whether a 17-year-old boy who suffered a severe allergic reaction to a home-delivered meal would have survived if he was given adrenaline sooner.

James Tsindos experienced anaphylaxis on the afternoon of May 27, 2021, after eating a burrito bowl ordered off the now-defunct Deliveroo app.

The meal contained a sauce made from cashews and James began experiencing allergy symptoms, including swollen lips, nausea, tingling in the throat and abdominal cramps.

Brighton Grammar student James Tsindos was a talented pianist. (60 Minutes)

His father called an ambulance and paramedics arrived at their Brighton home in Melbourne 's southeast about 2.50pm.

James received two doses of adrenaline five minutes apart and he was transferred to the nearby Holmesglen Private Hospital as a precaution.

As he arrived at the hospital about 3.44pm, he told the paramedics he was "wheezy" and he used his asthma puffer.

At 4.10pm, James' condition deteriorated and he was administered a third dose of adrenaline but he still had trouble breathing.

He was transferred to the resuscitation ward and within a minute became unresponsive before entering cardiac arrest.

Doctors and nurses tried to resuscitate James and he was transferred to The Alfred but never recovered.

His life support was turned off on May 29.

James' mother Venetia Tsindos. (Luis Enrique Ascui)

An inquest into James' death was held in October 2024 and coroner Sarah Gebert today delivered her findings.

She determined while James might have survived if he was administered the third dose of adrenaline sooner, she could not be certain.

Gerbert noted a panel of experts had reviewed the case and had differing opinions on James' ultimate prognosis.

The coroner said she could not side with a particular expert on whether James' death was preventable.

"I express my regret to the family that I am unable to do so," she said.

Gerbert made eight recommendations, including that the Department of Health update its guidelines around anaphylaxis management.

title: Robots perform backflips and advanced martial arts in stunning show
text:
Robots performing backflips and wielding nunchucks have wowed audiences during China's annual New Year Gala.

The humanoid robots performed for nearly five minutes on stage in a tightly choreographed routine that involved flips and dance moves, using various props, and even performing and interacting with child performers.

In the finale of the performance, a larger robot stood in the middle of the pack and held out a large sword while being elevated on a platform, with other robots around it waving staffs in the air.

READ MORE: ﻿ Tragic twist after Sydney chef dies in Bali

The robots performed alongside several child performers in a routine lasting almost five minutes. (CGTN)

The performance was designed to show how China's android and tech industries are rapidly advancing.

The robots in this particular routine came from a Chinese firm called Unitree Robitics, and several other companies showed off their latest android technologies at the four-hour-long gala to mark the Chinese New Year.

Some robots performed choreography as human pop stars, while others performed their own comedy skits.

China is the world's largest industrial robot market and home to more than half of all robots installed worldwide in 2024, according to the International Federation of Robotics.

A larger robot was the centre stage for the finale. (CGTN)

Robots and children perform at the Chinese New Year Gala in Beijing. (CGTN)

Beijing is also looking to use robots to help defend itself from manpower shortages and rising labour costs.

While humanoids have so far been most visible in novelty appearances and entertainment like Monday's show, they are also being piloted on assembly lines and in logistics hubs and science labs.

Even though on this occasion the robots were designed to look good and impress with their martial arts abilities, it could also be a sign of China flexing its muscle and looking to highlight its dominance in the field of robotics.

Reported with CNN ﻿

title: Council apologises after drivers overpay on hundreds of parking fines
text:
A﻿ Victorian council will roll out refunds after discovering an administrative error resulted in drivers overpaying on parking fines dating back to 2009.

Mansfield Shire Council, located north-east of Melbourne , has apologised to impacted motorists and said it is exploring a refund scheme for fines issued over a 10-year period between 2009 and 2019.

The council had been issuing parking infringements between 0.2 and 0.5 penalty units, which amounts to $41 and $102.﻿

Mansfield Shire Council overcharged drivers for parking fines between 2009 and 2019. (Supplied)

The Road Safety Act 1986 requires local government penalties to be set at the default $41 fine.

A formal council resolution is required before officers can issue the higher amount.﻿

Instead, the council had been issuing $102 infringement notices for 11 different types of infringements during this time period without a resolution.

"Unfortunately, the need for a formal resolution was not adequately communicated to councils in Victoria in 2009 or in 2019 when the guidelines were changed," Mansfield Shire Council said in a statement.

An estimated $34,588 in fines had been collected above the default penalty rate, which amounts to around 765 fines dating as far back as 2009.

Another $10,000 may have also been paid in legal costs or late fees.

Mayor Councillor Steve Rabie said the council is committed to rectifying the issue.

The Road Safety Act 1986 requires local government penalties to be set at the default $41 fine. (Google Maps)

"We are not scared to address errors. We've identified an issue and we will fix it," Rabie said.

"I have every confidence that the current team will work quickly to rectify these legacy issues. We know the community trusts us and we will work to maintain that trust."

"If this has affected anybody – we are sorry."

Rabie said the discrepancy was "disappointing because it's an avoidable situation".

﻿"It's a small amount of money over many years," he added.

"Mansfield Shire Council has not sought to make money on parking fines – we use fines to ensure fair use of parking resources for the community as a whole."

The council said it will continue to investigate the matter and has sought legal advice to confirm the amount that had been overpaid.

Drivers who are eligible for a refund should expect to be contacted.﻿

title: 'As good as it gets': Bank's bleak warning to every Australian
text:
The living standards of Australians in 2026 are "as good as it gets" unless the country can improve productivity, the boss of one of the nation's biggest banks has warned.﻿

Speaking to the Australian Financial Review's Chanticleer column , NAB chief executive Andrew Irvine said the Reserve Bank had no choice but to raise interest rates earlier this month , because a lack of productivity growth has put the handbrake on the national economy.

"The fact is, without productivity, Australia simply can't grow any faster than it is today," he told the publication.

Australians' living standards will be stuck in the mud without major economic reform, NAB has warned. (AFR/Louie Douvis)

"So how we're living now in 2026 is, frankly, as good as it gets, unless we lift productivity.

"This is peak Australia."

The nation's lagging productivity growth – an issue faced by most developed economies with the exception of the United States – has been highlighted as a key concern by scores of economists.

Last year, federal Treasurer Jim Chalmers held a three-day roundtable to canvas ideas to address the issue, and came out of the summit highlighting eight priority areas , including a widely supported road-user tax, where action would be taken quickly.

READ MORE: Man charged over alleged death threats to Treasurer Jim Chalmers ﻿

Treasurer Jim Chalmers is under pressure to deliver productivity-boosting measures. (Alex Ellinghausen)

The government also appears to be gearing up to hand down a significant, reform-filled federal budget, which may include changes to the contentious capital gains tax discount for property investors .

Chalmers has said the May 12 budget will include both productivity and savings measures.

Economic thinktank the e61 Institute today added its voice to the chorus calling for reform, releasing a new report which stated the tax system can't support current spending levels, and must be improved.

"There is no imminent debt crisis, but with renewed pressures ahead – like an ageing population and slow productivity growth – Australia's fiscal options are narrowing," chief executive Michael Brennan said.

COST OF LIVING: The super mistake which could cost young Aussies $128,000 ﻿

"With the tax system as it currently stands, we will be asking workers to pay a larger share of their income to fund a fiscal gap that has been building for two decades – and that's before any new spending commitments are added to the ledger," he added.

"Without tax reform, spending restraint will be needed to avoid burdening future generations with a fragile, inequitable and inefficient Australian economy."

In his comments to the AFR , Irvine said the upcoming budget was crucial, pinpointing housing and construction, energy prices, and private spending as particular areas to focus on.

NAB boss Andrew Irvine said the upcoming federal budget, to be delivered on May 12, will be a crucial moment. (Louis Trerise)

"The May budget is key, and the treasurer has indicated that we should have high expectations for that budget from a productivity perspective," he said.

"We're looking forward to hearing what he has to say."

Irvine's remarks were delivered on the same day NAB posted its first-quarter profit for the 2025-26 financial year.

Net profit was up 30 per cent year-on-year to $2.2 billion, which was welcomed by investors; the bank's share price rose some 4 per cent yesterday following the results announcement to reach a record high just north of $47.

title: Perth council offers $50 reward in new bin sorting crackdown
text:
A council in Perth is offering residents a $50 reward for sorting their rubbish correctly in a crackdown that will see bins checked and tagged.

Waste education officers from Victoria Park Council ﻿will conduct "quick and non-intrusive bin checks" on collection day to see whether there are any major sorting issues for the next six weeks.

They will then leave a feedback tag - either a happy face indicating a job well done or a sad face with tips on how to improve - on the bin lid. ﻿

Smiley face and sad face feedback will be left on bins. (Facebook)

"The tags include positive reinforcement for properly sorted bins, as well as helpful guidance on common errors to reduce recycling and organics contamination," the council said.

"This approach supports residents to sort their waste correctly by clarifying bin use in real time.﻿"

Victoria Park, located in the inner city, has a population of about 38,000 residents ﻿and covers about 1760 hectares.

Council will only select 10 per cent of ﻿household bins for the program.

It will also reward households that sort their waste correctly by placing them in a draw to win a $50 weekly prize and a $200 overall prize.

Residents are assured that ﻿the initiative is purely educational and no fines or penalties will be applied.

﻿Waste education officers will look at the rubbish at the very top of bins and write down feedback. (Facebook)

﻿Waste education officers will also not record any personal details or dig through bins.

Bin-tagging is part of a state government-funded ﻿initiative that sees councils receive up to $10,000 to roll out the measure.

The program has been shown to improve recycling rates by up to 25 per cent and reduce contamination by up to 60 per cent, which helps make the most out of the different bin systems and reduces landfill.

But the news gained mixed feedback from the Victoria Park community.

Some praised the idea, saying "﻿when we know better we can do better", while others claimed it was not worthwhile and a "waste of ratepayers' money".

title: WA councils could soon make laws to stop cats roaming
text:
The WA government has announced it is introducing legislation that would allow local governments to make laws limiting the movement of pet cats around the suburbs, to protect native birds and other wildlife.

Currently, local councils must apply to a state parliamentary committee if they want to make any kind of local laws to limit cat movements, and many have been knocked back.

Local Government Minister Hannah Beazley told Nadia Mitsopoulos on ABC Radio Perth the new law would empower local councils to be able to pass their own local laws to allow for cat containment to an owner's property.





Hannah Beazley announces plans to change the law to allow cat containment. (ABC News: Emma Wynne) Councils could also pass laws around cats in public areas.

"They could decide that cats are actually allowed out in public areas, but as long as they're under the effective control of the owner, whether that's in a cat carrier or on a lead or what have you," Ms Beazley said.

While cat containment rules would be up to local authorities to decide, Ms Beazley said the state government would look to offer guidelines for councils to consider.

"Hopefully once this passes both houses, we'll get together and put together a model local law, so there can be some consistency among councils if they follow that model local law.

"That can help them decide what infringements they might like to impose if someone is found to have a roaming cat."

The minister said she hoped councils around the state would go ahead with cat containment laws once able, saying it would benefit both pet cats and native wildlife.

"Cats who are contained to owners' properties, they live longer. And there's also evidence from the RSPCA to say that owners of contained cats spend about 400 per cent less on vet fees than roaming cats.

"In Australia, it is estimated that they do decimate around 300 million wildlife, in our country every year. I don't have an exact number for WA, but it isn't insignificant."

Cat containment would could require cats to be on a harness and leash if taken outside. (ABC Central West: Emily Middleton)

End to council frustration

Over the years, more than 20 local governments have attempted to bring in cat controls, but few have got them past the parliament's Joint Standing Committee on Delegated Legislation.

In 2025 The City of Bayswater had been attempting to bring in a local law to stop cats from wandering onto other people's properties or roaming unrestrained in public spaces.

The parliamentary committee rejected the city's request, and the committee's decision was upheld by WA's upper house, despite opposition from crossbench MPs.

Only Northam, Narrogin and Christmas Island have been successful in implementing cat containment laws.

Andrew Dover, chief executive of the shire of Pingelly in the Wheatbelt, has welcomed the proposed changes.

"We would like to bring in a local law containing cats and a requirement to be under effective control," Mr Dover said.

"The cat problem, I think, is not worse here than in other areas, but we do have a whole lot of wildlife that they can attack, so the results of the problem are quite bad here."

Councils could make laws requiring cats to be kept to homes or secure catios if the bill passes. (ABC News: Elloise Farrow-Smith)

The announcement has also been warmly welcomed by the WA Feral Cat working group.

Working group member Bruce Weber said that allowing councils to make their own laws would lead to quicker action than waiting for statewide cat containment laws.

"What the minister alluded to is that she was responding to the need for urgency for action here," Dr Weber said.

"If we had tried for uniformity across the entire state, that would have required a full review of the Cat Act and we wouldn't have landed a solution until well after 2030.

"The amendment is the quickest solution to get something in place that will allow us to have local governments implementing those laws sooner."

title: Breaking: Eric Dane, star of Grey's Anatomy and Euphoria, dies aged 53
text:
Eric Dane, a US actor who starred in some of the most successful TV series of the past 20 years, has died aged 53.

The star was best known for his role as Dr Mark Sloan in long-running medical drama Grey's Anatomy, and as the patriarch of the Jacobs family in teen hit Euphoria.

He also had notable parts in the films Burlesque, Marley and Me, and Bad Boys: Ride or Die.

His representatives said Dane died from amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, known also as Lou Gehrig's disease, less than a year after he announced his diagnosis.

ALS is the most common form of motor neurone disease, a degenerative disorder that causes progressive paralysis of the muscles.

"He spent his final days surrounded by dear friends, his devoted wife, and his two beautiful daughters, Billie and Georgia, who were the center of his world," the representatives said in a statement that requested privacy for his family.

"Throughout his journey with ALS, Eric became a passionate advocate for awareness and research, determined to make a difference for others facing the same fight.

"He will be deeply missed, and lovingly remembered always. Eric adored his fans and is forever grateful for the outpouring of love and support he's received."

title: Algal bloom report lashed as 'vanilla' by scientist turned political candidate
text:
Fishing licence buybacks and restocking plans are among the 11 recommendations in the final report of the state parliament's Joint Committee on Harmful Algal Blooms in South Australia.

The committee, which was chaired by Greens MLC Robert Simms, was established in September.

The report, which was handed down this week, follows a similar one conducted last year after a Senate inquiry.

The bloom is not currently impacting metropolitan beaches but is still in other parts of the state. (ABC South East SA: Caroline Horn)

The report also recommended a long-term assessment program of the ecological effects of the ongoing bloom, as well as a review of staffing across agencies to ensure "any gaps in water monitoring and research capacity are appropriately filled", and that those agencies implement regular reporting regimes.

While now largely absent from metropolitan beaches and monitoring sites, the algal bloom is still impacting other areas in the state.

Seafood Industry South Australia told the committee the harmful algal bloom had had "catastrophic impacts on South Australia's seafood sector". (ABC News: Caroline Horn)

Buybacks 'needed'

The committee's report recommends South Australia's next government consider introducing a fishery licence buyback scheme for impacted fishers, with a limited number made available at low or no cost for fishers who choose to temporarily leave the industry, allowing them to re-enter when stocks improve.

The report recommended the government consider a commercial fishing licence buyback scheme. (Des Woolford: user submitted)

In its submission to the committee, the industry body, Seafood Industry South Australia, said the algal bloom had been catastrophic and the "greatest ecological, economic and social challenge faced by South Australia's seafood industry in a generation".

As well as buyback implementation, licence rationalisation and resource reallocation, the industry called for investment in calamari, whiting, garfish and snapper units across Gulf St Vincent and Spencer Gulf.

Port Wakefield-based marine scale fisherman Bart Butson told the ABC's Country Hour that a buyback scheme was "much needed and wanted".

Bart Butson also gave evidence to the Senate inquiry into the algal bloom in Ardrossan. (ABC News: Viki Ntafillis)

"Fishers … know how slow the recovery of the fish stock is, so they know it's a long time before we'll be properly commercial fishing again, so there's still some desperation out there amongst the fishers," Mr Butson said.

" [A buyback scheme] would mean different things to different people — if a fisher is older, he may want to slip into retirement, but if a fisher is younger, he may want to pay off some debt and start work in a different career. "

In late 2025, the South Australian opposition pledged a $21-million voluntary fisheries buyback scheme — if elected at the upcoming state election — to allow commercial fishers to leave the industry amid the harmful algal bloom.

Primary Industries Minister Clare Scriven said the Labor government had invested $500,000 to investigate the feasibility of a buyback scheme.

South Australian Primary Industries Minister Clare Scriven. (ABC South East SA: Caroline Horn)

"We wanted to invest the time and resources to actually make sure this is looked at properly so we can be sure that there is a sustainable fishing industry going forward … in terms of fish stocks, but also in terms of financial stability for those in the industry," Ms Scriven said.

Dissenting statements

The joint committee's report also contained dissenting statements from the chair, Robert Simms, and Liberal MPs Nicola Centofanti and Matt Cowdrey.

Liberal Shadow Minister for Primary Industries Nicola Centofanti. (ABC Riverland: Will Hunter)

In their statement, Ms Centofanti and Mr Cowdrey said the report had not adequately confronted the "scale of government failure", including gaps in baseline monitoring and delays in public communication.

Mr Simms said while he endorsed all of the report's recommendations, he had concerns that it did not contain findings that explicitly highlighted "deficiencies in the State Government's response" given what he called a "weight of evidence" presented to the committee that it had been too slow to respond to the bloom and had significant gaps in data collection.

'Vanilla' recommendations

Ecologist and now candidate for the upper house in the upcoming March 21 state election, Faith Coleman, told ABC Radio yesterday that the recommendations were "all pretty vanilla".

Faith Coleman says the report's recommendations were "all pretty vanilla". (ABC News: Che Chorley)

Ms Coleman appeared before the committee in January and raised allegations that public servants had told her they had been told not to investigate the cause of the algal bloom until after the state election.

Those allegations have been strongly refuted by the South Australian government.

She said she was concerned that the report did not reflect concerns raised in the community around a lack of access to data, including those gathered in the lead-up to the discovery of the bloom.

"There's a series of conversations around a lack of transparency that haven't been addressed in the recommendations of that report," she said.

The South Australian election will be held on March 21.

title: University secures 'conditional approval' after research accreditation issue, internal emails reveal
text:
The newly merged Adelaide University has experienced an accreditation and compliance issue for human research projects, only receiving "conditional" accreditation as opposed to full approval from a federal body late last week with further documentation still required.

The Human Research Ethics Committees [HRECs] need accreditation from the National Health and Medical Research Council [NHMRC] to proceed with research involving people.

HRECs must also be registered with the NHMRC to review and monitor clinical trials of unregistered therapeutic goods.

Adelaide University opened to students this year following the merger of the University of Adelaide and the University of South Australia.

Internal email recognises delays

In an internal email circulated to some people at the university last week and seen by the ABC, prior to the conditional approval being handed down, it stated that Adelaide University was "unable to allocate human research ethic projects to an HREC".

"The delays are not a matter of resourcing, but of compliance … we are aware there are many time critical impacts, stressors and uncertainties due to the delays", the email read.

Its author adding that they recognised people were dealing with "the impacts of the transition and general delays".

" We acknowledge that current delays and uncertainty around human research ethics processes are impacting research planning, delivery, and student activities. We appreciate how frustrating this is and understand the urgency many researchers are experiencing. "

It added that the Animal Ethics, and Institutional Biosafety Committee, which are separate to Human Research Ethics, were "tracking well".

In a statement to the ABC, an Adelaide University spokesperson later said it has "not led to any delays in ethics approvals".

"Research can be conducted at Adelaide University because the committees can review the same projects under conditional registration as they would be able to do under full registration," they said.

"Ongoing research approved under ethics committees at University of Adelaide and University of South Australia continues under these historical approvals and has been unaffected by this process."

Adelaide University was created when the University of Adelaide and Uni SA merged. (ABC News: Briana Fiore)

It said full accreditation would be achieved when the university completes "minor amendments to the wording in its policies and terms of reference".

An Adelaide University spokesperson said the "conditional" approval was handed down on February 13 and that the committee can review projects and "research can be conducted at Adelaide University".

An NHMRC spokesperson said it had approved two of the university's HRECs "subject to the university making administrative updates to their documents in the coming months".

"NHMRC expects that the Adelaide University HRECs will commence a review of new ethics applications shortly," a spokesperson said.

Do you know more? Contact reporter Briana Fiore at fiore.briana@abc.net.au

Students raise concerns

A student who spoke to the ABC on the condition on anonymity said not getting the accreditation in a timely manner was "a most basic failing".

They said ethics approvals were needed to make sure the research students were doing was done in an ethical way where humans weren't being exploited or being exposed to unnecessary harm.

They added that medical research could include things such as trialling a brand-new cancer drug.

"It could [have] potentially delayed students that are trying to work within their time frames to do their research degrees," the source said.

The student said grants were also tied to a very strict timeline.

"We have not really been able to contact the ethics department, so no one really knows what's going on at all."

"It's sort of unbelievable that this could be an oversight that gets made," the source said.

Following questions from the ABC, the university updated students via email.

The accreditation issue follows students having difficulties with enrolments and receiving timely communication from the uni.

title: Olympic final? Indra Brown's still got maths homework to do
text:
There can't be too many people in Livigno as relaxed as Indra Brown appears to be this week.

The 16-year-old has just completed her first two competitive runs at an Olympic Games just months after making her FIS World Cup debut.

Not only that, she's qualified fourth for Saturday night's final.

For most it would be surreal to even comprehend.

The atmosphere at the Livigno Snow Park made it even more so.

Indra Brown did not let the occasion daunt her. (Supplied: AOC/Chris Hocking)

The snow that teemed down in a torrent for almost 24 hours had slowed to a gentle dusting, each frozen flake glittering ethereally under the blinding lights of Livigno's spectacular arena.

It's fairytale stuff.

And yet, despite all this, Brown can't escape reality completely.

"I actually did some maths homework earlier today," Brown told media after her second run.

"So, you know, I'm keeping up with it, but try and enjoy the Olympics as much as I can as well."

Get the latest: Winter Olympics day 13: Young Aussie star reaches halfpipe final, Liu caps comeback after retiring at 16 Stay across the highs and lows of the Games with the ABC Sport Daily podcast and ABC Sport.

It wasn't just any maths homework either, but linear equations.

Brown noted that the halfpipe was "definitely" easier than maths, although the way she reeled off her trick sequence without hesitation later in the interview felt as if it were an equation all of its own, one that Brown and Australia hope will lead to an Olympic medal.

Brown was second out of the gate on Thursday night, the FIS World Cup leader following defending champion and superstar Eileen Gu.

She might not like maths, but the numbers say Indra Brown is a chance for a medal on Saturday night. (Supplied: AOC/Chris Hocking)

Incredibly, Gu crashed on her first run, a minor error on her third hit seeing her clip the lip of the halfpipe and lose her ski.

Undaunted by the pressure, Brown laid down a superb run, scoring 80.75.

Then, as if to underline just how completely in control she is, the teenage star completed an even better run second time around, adding a more technically difficult penultimate hit to score 87.50 and sit as fourth-best qualifier for the final (Sunday at 5:30am AEDT).

Given the horrific accident to Canadian Cassie Sharpe, who underwent lengthy treatment in the pipe after getting knocked out and taken away on a stretcher, Brown will likely be the third-highest qualifier.

"To be able to put two runs down in my first Olympics is just a relief," Brown said.

"There's definitely nerves that go through. I want to do my best and show everyone what I can do.

"So landing that first one, the relief it gives you is just so amazing.

"The pressure was off, I knew I've got a score down, so I can really increase my amp [amplitude], add a new trick, and just see how much I can push it.

"I'm just enjoying it so much."

Indra Brown has got plenty to look forward to this week. (Supplied: AOC/Chris Hocking)

Snow had fallen so intensely over the past 24 hours there were fears qualification may be delayed, adding to the lengthy congestion of events building up in Livigno as the Games reaches its conclusion.

Instead, the pipe had been well cleaned, a relief for an athlete the size of Brown who already struggles lower down the pipe on account of not being able to generate enough momentum.

She simply doesn't weigh enough or have the strength in her 16-year-old body that her rivals can call upon.

But that didn't matter either.

"They've done an amazing job clearing out all the snow," Brown said.

"It definitely wasn't as fast as it could be, but they've done an amazing job to keep it top notch."

Will Indra Brown be celebrating a medal on Saturday? (Supplied: AOC/Chris Hocking)

Brown had raised expectations of a surprise medal by enjoying one of the greatest starts to a World Cup career in FIS history, collecting medals as readily as she has been collecting pins in the athlete's village.

"I have been on the pin trading game," Brown said with joyous enthusiasm.

"I think they're all so unique and different in their own ways, so I just love collecting them."

The then-15-year-old won a bronze medal at her first World Cup event at Secret Garden in China and followed it up with a silver medal at Copper Mountain in the US, before becoming Australia's youngest World Cup winner with a gold in Calgary.

The 15yo schoolgirl who has taken the Freeski world by storm When 15-year-old Indra Brown reached the podium in her first World Cup event, she needed help from global superstar Eileen Gu to pop the cork on her champagne. She's had plenty more practice since, as she heads into the Milan Cortina 2026 Winter Olympic Games.

Brown's youthful naivety touched Gu on that first podium in Secret Garden, with the Chinese superstar helping the young Aussie get the cork off her celebratory champagne.

"It was so sweet," Gu said.

"The same thing happened when I was her, aged 15 on my first World Cup podium, and I couldn't open it and no-one helped me. I wish someone had.

"It's so wonderful to see the next generation of the sport … so I'm excited to watch her progress."

A fourth-placed finish at Buttermilk followed, before she claimed a silver medal in her first X-Games at Aspen in the weeks before the Olympics.

Indra Brown says there is no pressure on her to win a medal. (Supplied: AOC/Chris Hocking)

"I'm just going to go out there and try to enjoy myself," Brown said.

"Not put any pressure or any thoughts over a certain result.

"I want to just go out there, ski in my first Olympic finals at my first Olympics and enjoy it because it's something that doesn't come very often."

Loading...

There will almost certainly be more homework to complete before she gets there, despite her admitting that her schoolmates in Melbourne — who filed in early to watch her compete this morning — were amazing.

"There might be, probably will [be more homework]" Brown said, still beaming.

"Not too excited for it, but I've got to get it done.

"My school is really supportive of me and not expecting me to have every bit of work done as soon as it comes."

An Olympic final is probably a good enough excuse to be a little late, to be honest.

title: Broncos fall short of another miracle comeback in World Club Challenge
text:
Hull KR have hung on to beat Brisbane and win their maiden World Club Challenge 30-24 after thwarting a late comeback by the visitors in freezing conditions.

The Robins deserved their victory and the title of the planet's greatest club rugby league side after dominating for the majority of the clash at Hull's MKM Stadium.

It was a triumph for the ages for coach Willie Peters, whose team had been written off before the match by pundits in Australia.

Peters and his team have now won the quadruple — the Super League major and minor premiership, the Challenge Cup title and the World Club Challenge.

It was five tries each with Broncos captain Adam Reynolds landing just two from five conversion attempts, his last two misses proving crucial.

The Broncos fell just short of another stunning comeback. (Getty Images: Alex Livesey)

The Robins led 30-4 in the 57th minute after Peta Hiku followed a Tyrone May grubber.

The Broncos, famous for their three comeback wins in the finals series last year to win the NRL title, clicked into gear.

"It was the longest 20-odd minutes of my life," a delighted Peters said.

"I am just really happy with the way they scrambled, fought and dug in to win.

"We were the underdogs. Not many people thought we could win."

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Bench prop Ben Talty burst through to put Pat Carrigan over and a minute later winger Deine Mariner streaked down the right touchline.

Reece Walsh threw a corker to put centre Gehamat Shibasaki over and the Broncos had three tries in five minutes.

Robins five-eighth Mikey Lewis failed to find touch from a penalty at the death and the Broncos went the length through Kotoni Staggs to set up a thrilling final three minutes.

But Michael Maguire's side could not pull off another miracle.

The ABC of SPORT Sports content to make you think... or allow you not to. A newsletter delivered each Saturday.

AAP

title: A blow to Trump could be a $1.4bn windfall for Australian businesses
text:
Australian exporters to the United States could be set for a windfall if some of the Trump administration's trade tariffs are ruled unlawful, a major consulting firm has found.

Modelling by EY Australia shows Australian exporters would be owed more than $1.4 billion in refunds collectively if the tariffs are struck down, with a decision possible when the US's top court sits in the early hours of Saturday morning.

The Supreme Court of the United States (SCOTUS) will determine whether the president had the power to impose tariffs almost globally using the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), after lower courts found he did not.

EY partner Luke Branson, who leads the firm's Oceania Global Trade practice, said the decision related to a 10 per cent tariff imposed on most Australian goods last April.

"Legal analysts are doubtful about the powers under this particular legislation providing the president the authority to impose tariffs," Mr Branson told the ABC.

"This particular statute allows the president to regulate imports … but it doesn't explicitly say that the president can impose tariffs."

Luke Branson says the onus will be on Australian exporters to seek out refunds. (Supplied)

The sectors most affected by the IEEPA tariffs include manufactured goods like industrial machinery and medical devices, and agricultural exports such as beef, beverages and horticulture, according to the firm.

Vekta is a Perth-based company that makes robotic machinery used to build homes in the US, which is its largest market, generating up to 60 per cent of its revenue.

"We'll definitely be watching the news and keeping our eye on that Supreme Court ruling," managing director Ed Serrano said.

" We're hoping we're going to see a little bit of a reprieve. "

The business has had to partly absorb the 10 per cent tariffs on its multi-million-dollar orders — not to mention the loss of potential customers.

Loading...

"The direct cost to the business from those baseline tariffs is multiple hundreds of thousands of dollars," Mr Serrano said.

"It definitely has impacted our sales opportunities in the US."

Other tariffs remain

While the removal of IEEPA tariffs would provide some relief, certain Australian exports are subject to larger duties under other US laws.

The so-called "section 232 tariffs", which were imposed under the Trade Expansion Act, are as high as 50 per cent for steel and aluminium products.

The beef industry has been hit hard by the tariffs. (Supplied: Dalene Wray)

Under the same regime, a 25 per cent tariff applies to Australian-made automotive parts and certain wooden products like kitchen cabinets.

Adding to the woes of companies like Vekta, US authorities are investigating the effect of certain imports on national security, which could result in even higher tariffs.

"For example, they're looking at putting up to potentially a 50 per cent tax on all industrial equipment," Mr Serrano said.

"That's going to virtually destroy any chance of us selling our equipment into the US market."

Ed Serrano says his company is losing market share in Australia because of tax-free imports from the US. (ABC News: Armin Azad)

On top of that, Mr Serrano said Vekta was losing market share in Australia because of tax-free imports from the US.

"Under the current investigations, we may see all of our equipment produced in Perth is taxed at up to 50 per cent," he said.

"And yet, when … our competitors import their products from the US into Australia, that's at zero per cent.

"I can't say that I've heard of anything that the Australian government has done to help alleviate this problem for Australian manufacturers."

A spokesperson for the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade told the ABC that the government would continue to advocate for the tariff removal.

"Australia, at every level, including the Prime Minister, continues to use every engagement opportunity to advocate for the removal of these unjustified and unwarranted tariffs," they said.

Trade Minister Don Farrell did not respond to the ABC's request for comment.

Refunds not automatic

Mr Branson stressed that if the court found the tariffs were invalid, the onus would be on Australian exporters to seek out refunds.

"They'll have to lodge a claim in effect where they've calculated the duties that they have paid," he said.

"There's no automatic refund."

Even if the court strikes down the tariffs, it is thought the administration may seek to avoid paying back exporters.

"There's a lot of talk by legal analysts in the United States that the president might impose other measures … under other legal avenues, lawful legal avenues that he has used in the past, to basically backdate tariffs so that he doesn't have to provide refunds," Mr Branson said.

title: Pacific countries to use Tuvalu's plight as example to world powers
text:
Fiji, Palau and Tuvalu look set to host world leaders and climate change ministers in the lead-up to COP31 in a bid to encourage larger countries to accelerate cuts to emissions.

Both Fiji and Palau had offered to host the "pre-COP" meetings secured for the Pacific under a complicated deal struck last year that saw hosting rights for the main climate summit go to Türkiye while giving Climate Change Minister Chris Bowen a key position in global climate negotiations.

Australia and Pacific Islands nations have declared they want to use the opportunity to publicise the Pacific's existential battle with climate change.

But negotiations on who should host events in the lead-up to the summit have dragged on.

Palau President Surangel Whipps Jr is hosting the Pacific Islands Forum meeting in early September. (Reuters: David Dee Delgado)

Palau President Surangel Whipps Jr told the ABC that Pacific leaders were close to landing a deal that would hand Palau, Fiji and Tuvalu a prominent role ahead of COP31.

Palau is already hosting the Pacific Islands Forum (PIF) meeting in early September.

Under that deal Mr Whipps would also invite other global leaders to a high-level climate summit around the same time.

Australia backs Pacific Islands facing China heat over Taiwan Beijing reprimanded Pacific Island nation ambassadors for including Taiwan in the regional summit last month, but Australia and New Zealand have lent their support.

Mr Whipps said the climate meeting in Palau would be a good "launching board" to build momentum for COP climate negotiations through the back half of the year, while also drawing new commitments from global leaders to help Pacific nations decarbonise and build infrastructure to withstand climate impacts.

"At the PIF our priorities are going to be 100 per cent renewables, the ocean-climate nexus and … accelerating investments that build resilience from climate change," he told the ABC's Pacific Beat.

"We believe these topics are important outcomes we can come out of PIF with.

" And if we bring other high leaders in, then that can help springboard that message. We're adding another dimension to really maximise that impact. "

Under the proposal, Fiji would host the traditional pre-COP meeting with climate change ministers in October.

Mr Whipps said that meeting would likely include a trip to the low-lying island nation of Tuvalu, which is extremely vulnerable to sea-level rise and has played an outsized role in Pacific climate advocacy for years.

Pre-COP plans 'close to a resolution'

Mr Bowen said Pacific discussions on the pre-COP events were "very close to a resolution" and he expected an announcement "very, very soon".

"It's an important opportunity to bring senior climate ministers and others from around the world to the Pacific to see firsthand the impacts of climate change," he said.

Government canvasses Pacific leaders on backdown option for COP31 The federal government has begun to contact Pacific leaders to canvass a COP31 "Plan B" which would see Adelaide abandon its bid for the global United Nations climate summit so long as Pacific nations are guaranteed a role in next year's conference.

Mr Whipps said he had also invited US Vice-President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio to the PIF leaders' meeting and was hopeful one of them would be able to attend.

Palau has close links to the United States as one of three Compact of Free Association states that accept substantial American funding in exchange for guaranteeing US military access.

But Mr Whipps acknowledged he was unlikely to be able to secure US participation in the climate meeting given the Trump administration's withdrawal from the Paris Agreement and its hostility to decarbonisation.

title: Quick hits: Aussie channels Wolf of Wall Street and Swedish curlers battle the world
text:
JD Vance wishes an American-born Chinese superstar chose to represent the US and snowboarder Scotty James shares a message of defiance.

Here are the quick hits from day 13 of the Winter Olympics.

1.Swedes claim curling world was 'against us'

Sweden finished well outside the medals in the men's curling, with its Olympic campaign overshadowed by controversy.

The defending champions — who failed to qualify for the semifinals — had accused Canada of cheating by double-touching stones during a game last week.

The Swedish men's team feels the cheating controversy did not help the cause. (AP: Misper Apawu)

Swedish skipper Niklas Edin admits the incident, which attracted an angry denial from Canada, contributed to his team's poor showing.

He says he was shocked by the response, claiming it was just an inoffensive dig at the Canadians.

"Obviously what happened in the game against Canada took a lot of focus off, there were a lot of negative comments here and there," Edin said.

"We didn't really expect that to happen. We just meant to do a ‌friendly comment like, 'Just let go of ‌the rock.'

"But obviously that took a really strange turn and turned half of the curling world against us. ‌It's been tough for sure.

"We wish we could start over and do it again and would have let things kind of unfold differently, I hope."

2. Aussie debuts in sport she taught herself

Lara Hamilton faced more challenges than most in getting to the start line of the inaugural Olympic skimo competition.

Taking part in an event dominated by well-resourced Europeans nations, Hamilton made her Olympics debut only four months after getting a specialised skimo coach.

Lara Hamilton competed in the women's skimo event at the Milano Cortina Games. (AP: Gabriele Facciotti)

She had largely taught herself the sport until that point, having begun her journey using 1990s-era equipment.

Hamilton was also forced to fund some of her preparation for the Games with a part-time job and occasional gigs as a DJ.

When speaking to the Nine Network after finishing sixth in her heat, Hamilton pointed to some of the hurdles she faced in making it the Olympics.

"If I started on a pair of 1998 frame bindings and with no coach until October of last year — [I] taught myself how to do it — you can do it," Hamilton said.

3. Vance wishes Gu chose the US

Eileen Gu is a genuine superstar of the freestyle skiing world, having already won two medals in Italy to go with the three she claimed in 2022.

Born and raised in California to an American father and Chinese mother, Gu is multilingual and says she embraces both sides of her identity, but has chosen to represent China.

Eileen Gu of China has won two silvers and is going for gold in the women's freeski halfpipe. (Getty Images: Patrick Smith)

Speaking to The Athletic, Gu said her decision to ski for China was motivated by the opportunity to "have a much greater impact" than she would competing for the US, citing a lack of "idols" for young Chinese skiers.

But in an interview with Fox News, US Vice-President JD Vance suggested Gu should be competing for the country of her birth.

"I certainly think that somebody who grew up in the United States of America, who benefited from our education system, from the freedoms and liberties that make this country a great place — I would hope that they want to compete with the United States of America," he said.

"So I'm going to root for American athletes. I think part of that is people who identify themselves as Americans. That's who I'm rooting for in this Olympics."

4. American skier avoids leaf-blower disaster

Ben Loomis narrowly avoided disaster on day 13. (Getty Images: Tom Weller)

We've witnessed a collection of harrowing prangs over the past couple weeks, with Lindsey Vonn's heartbreaking fall in the women's downhill chief amongst them.

On day 13, US skier Ben Loomis narrowly avoided adding what would have been one of the more bizarre accidents of the 2026 Games.

Competing in the ski jumping component of the nordic combined team sprint, Loomis was flying down the slope in horrendous conditions, which were so bad that organisers had been given leaf blowers to clear the track between jumps.

And whether it was through some poor communication or a lapse in concentration, a solitary leaf blower was left dangling in Loomis's path.

Vision showed Loomis being clipped by the leaf blower moments before executing the jump.

"I've never experienced anything like this," Loomis told Norwegian outlet NRK.

"Fortunately, it happened at the top, so I just tried to convince myself to make a good jump."

And to his credit he did, registering a distance of 116 metres and opting against retaking the jump.

The US finished seventh in the event.

5. James 'ain't f***in' leaving'

One of Australia's greatest Winter Olympians of all time, snowboarder Scotty James, entered rarefied air earlier this week, claiming a third Games medal in the men's halfpipe.

However, it was a bittersweet result for James, who, in notching the second silver of his Olympic career in Livigno, was unable to claim the gold for which he had so long pined.

"It's a bit numb," James said in the aftermath of the event.

"Like, I don't really know how to feel."

On Thursday, however, James took to social media with a much more defiant message, pledging to continue his pursuit of that illusive gold medal in four years time.

Loading Instagram content

"So to anyone who's ever fallen just short, I see you. It's okay to feel the sting. It means you care. It means you dared to want something extraordinary," James wrote.

"I'm proud. I'm hungry. I'm grateful.

"And… I ain't f***in' leaving! See you at the next one."

title: Cassie Sharpe's horrific crash a stark reminder of Winter Olympics dangers
text:
The atmosphere at almost every Winter Olympics event in Livigno has been festival-like.

Pumping music accompanies the athletes' runs, and fans bob, weave and cheer as each competitor takes their turn.

Australia's Indra Brown, only 16, had just completed her run in the halfpipe and was cheerfully recounting it to an assortment of Australia's press.

Behind her, Canadian veteran Cassie Sharpe dropped into the pipe.

Get the latest: Winter Olympics day 13: Young Aussie star reaches halfpipe final, Liu caps comeback after retiring at 16 Stay across the highs and lows of the Games with the ABC Sport Daily podcast and ABC Sport.

The 33-year-old is one of the sport's greats, having won gold in PyeongChang in 2018 and silver in 2022.

After winning that silver medal in Beijing, Sharpe took time off to start a family. In 2023, she gave birth to a daughter, Louella, or Lou for short, before coming back to chase the feeling of being an Olympic champion once more.

Cassie Sharpe was sitting second after the first round of qualifying. (Getty Images: Cameron Spencer)

Despite her time away from competition — three years in total — Sharpe came into these Games a genuine medal chance.

Those hopes ended in sickening fashion in qualifying on Thursday night, local time.

Towards the end of her second run, Sharpe hit the lip of the pipe and slammed into the rock-hard ice with a terrifying force.

The 33-year-old slapped down on her right side, her skis and poles scattered behind her like the splintered detritus of some awful calamity.

Which, of course, they were.

Cassie Sharpe crashed hard in the second run of ski halfpipe qualifying. (Getty Images: Adam Pretty)

She tumbled down the seven-metre-high walls to the centre of the pipe, coming to a halt, face down.

There she remained, motionless.

Her coach, watching at the bottom of the pipe, instantly set off at a run to get to his skier, with medical staff also leaping into action.

The pulsating party music was instantly turned down, its glaring incongruity rightly dispensed with, leaving a harrowing silence allowing the worst possible thoughts unfettered access to play across our minds.

Medical staff were quick to arrive to Cassie Sharpe, but patient when treating her. (Getty Images: Adam Pretty)

The crowd silently watched on, muted by anxiety and paralysed by concern, as Sharp lay motionless.

Some turned away as she appeared to start to convulse as the medical staff reached her.

It was impossible not to think of her family, two-year-old Lou — likely to be oblivious to her mother's plight, thank goodness — her husband, former freestyle skier Justin Dorey, who most certainly was not.

Cassie Sharpe's daughter, Louella, is now two years old. (Getty Images: Ezra Shaw)

He would know, as so many have learned this past two weeks, that injury is simply the reality of winter sport, a reality that has been exposed time and time again at these Games.

Never are these daredevil athletes more than a handful of seconds away from disaster, millimetres from utter catastrophe.

The risks that every single one of them takes in pursuit of excellence come ever closer to being unconscionable, yet to deny them the chance to pursue it would be just as bad.

Skiers and snowboarders are only ever seconds from disaster in Livigno. (Getty Images: Patrick Smith)

Just as Lindsey Vonn was never going to let her ACL injury stop her from competing here, none of these athletes is ever going to play it safe when the reward is a brush with Olympic immortality.

But equally, just as the crowd at the base of the Cortina ski centre watched in horror at Vonn's silent screams on the big screens, at the Livigno Snow Park, the drama unfolded without the insulating protection of a screen.

The crowd here had an unfiltered view of the most upsetting of scenes.

The tense wait for any movement was agonising.

Canadian teammate Rachael Karker, who had just completed her second run moments before, looked in clear distress in the mix zone.

Defending champion Gu Ailing broke off an interview with NBC to check what was happening.

But the cheer that erupted when Sharpe raised a gloved hand as she was carted away on a stretcher, her head secured by a neck brace, was one of abject relief.

Cassie Sharpe's waves were a welcome relief to spectators. (Getty Images: Cameron Spencer)

Those painful moments before were being banished by those cheering voices just as quickly as Sharpe herself was being ferried away down the slope from the halfpipe to a waiting ambulance.

Moments later, Zhang Kexin was dropping in for her second run, and the music was back up and blaring.

Loading...

Canadian broadcaster CBC reported that Sharpe had lost consciousness, before the Canadian Olympic Committee released a statement saying that the two-time Olympic medallist was in a stable condition and was being evaluated by medical staff.

Her score from her first run was enough to see her qualify in third place for Saturday's final.

Whether she will be there will come down to the opinion of medical experts.

title: Australian company wins prestigious international dance award
text:
Bangarra Dance Theatre, Australia's leading Indigenous dance company, has won the prestigious international award, the Golden Lion, at the Venice Biennale.

The prize for lifetime achievement in dance, first awarded in 1995, will be presented during the international festival of contemporary dance, Venice Danza, which kicks off in July.

Mirning choreographer Frances Rings, the artistic director of Bangarra, has described the win as a "validation of the important role of Bangarra in bringing First Nations stories to the stage and the responsibility that we carry".

"It's quite emotional because Bangarra doesn't do what it does to receive awards," she told ABC Arts.

"The reward for us is the validation from our communities and from our Elders, and when we see our audiences' perceptions change and shift and they become our allies and support First Nations people."

The company's win follows Back to Back Theatre, a disability-led theatre group from Geelong, taking home the Golden Lion at Venice's international theatre festival in 2024.

In the same year, Bigambul and Kamilaroi artist Archie Moore won the Golden Lion at the Venice Art Biennale.

The win at the Venice Biennale makes Bangarra the first company and first First Nations performers to receive the award since it was first presented.

Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander readers are advised that this article contains names of people who have died.

Award-winning British choreographer Wayne McGregor, the outgoing director of Venice Danza, described Bangarra in a statement as a company that has "caused a seismic shift in our understanding of dance and the cultural context in which it is performed".

"Their integrity, passion, commitment and power have often driven radical change, frequently overcoming great challenge and resistance," he said.

Bangarra's 2023 show Yuldea was inspired by Yooldil Kapi on the edge of the Nullarbor, an ancient water soak ravaged by industry and nuclear testing. (Supplied: Bangarra Dance Theatre/Daniel Boud)

Previous recipients of the Golden Lion for dance include late American dancer Merce Cunningham, German choreographer Pina Bausch and American choreographer William Forsythe.

'We still have work to do'

Bangarra — whose name means "to make fire" in Wiradjuri language — was established in 1989 by the late Gumbaynggirr dancer and Vietnam veteran Rob Bryant, African American dancer Carole Y Johnson and South African dancer Cheryl Stone.

Johnson was also a co-founder of the National Aboriginal and Islander Skills Association (NAISDA) dance school.

In 1991, Stephen Page, a Nunukul/Ngugi man of the Quandamooka peoples and a Munaldjali man of the Yugambeh nation, became artistic director, running the company until 2022.

"We've been using our platform to share our stories and working with communities for over 37 years now," Rings said.

"But we still have a lot of work to do, because our stories are out of sight — they're in communities, in the memories of our Elders, to be unearthed still, and in the untold shadow of our nation's history.

" Our role as truth-tellers is to give a platform and voice to those stories and create a better understanding and awareness so that we get constructive change that benefits our people and communities. "

Bangarra is best known for its performance of Bennelong in 2017 and Dark Emu in 2018, a dance adaptation of Bruce Pascoe's book. It has toured the world, including London, Paris, New York and Tokyo.

For their 2025 show Illume, Bangarra collaborated with Goolarrgon Bard artist Darrell Sibosado, who combines pearl shell carving with installation art. (Supplied: Bangarra Dance Theatre/Daniel Boud)

Its process for making work is culturally embedded, with choreographers and other creatives often visiting Indigenous communities multiple times to better understand their stories.

"Terrain's really a timeless work because Country's always going to be a priority for Indigenous people. It's always going to be the cornerstone of our existence," Rings told ABC Arts in 2022.

Taking Bangarra global

At Venice Danza in July, Bangarra is set to perform Terrain, choreographed by Rings in 2012, which examines the impact of colonisation on the natural environment, and the important role that Indigenous peoples and knowledges play in caring for Country.

Bangarra's Illume a 'glowing bridge between worlds' Mirning choreographer Frances Rings and Goolarrgon Bard artist Darrell Sibosado collaborate on Bangarra Dance Theatre's new production.

Rings is proud to share Terrain with an international audience, working with Bangarra's company of dancers to bring it to life.

"It's a whole new generation of dancers that will be performing this so they inherit this role from the previous dancers," she said.

"It is a homage to Country and First Nations peoples' relationship to Country, what it means to wear Country as a second skin, and how to give insight into that relationship of Country to audiences and to our broader society.

" The messages in that work are still relevant today and more important than ever. "

The company will premiere its next show Flora with Australian Ballet in Melbourne in March, before touring Sydney, with the show Sheltering also visiting Brisbane and Canberra from May.

In Flora, 35 dancers, alongside Kalkangu musician William Barton and Meriam Samsep designer Grace Lillian Lee, will bring to life the evolution of Australian plants and demonstrate the resilience of Indigenous peoples.

Sheltering brings together three Bangarra works. Two were born from the company's emerging artist festival Dance Clan: Grounded by Indjalandji-Dhidhanu and Alyewarre woman Glory Tuohy-Daniell; and the dance movie Brown Boys by Gamilaroi and Tongan man Daniel Mateo and Cass Mortimer. The final work on the bill is Sheoak by Rings, which premiered in 2015.

title: Bill Gates pulls out of India AI summit amid Epstein scrutiny
text:
Bill Gates has pulled out of India's AI Impact Summit hours before his scheduled keynote address as scrutiny over his ties to late financier and sex offender Jeffrey Epstein intensified following the release of US Justice Department emails.

The abrupt withdrawal of Microsoft's co-founder dealt a fresh blow to a flagship event already marred by organisational lapses, a robot row and complaints of traffic chaos.

The six-day event still notched more than $US200 billion ($284 billion) in investment pledges for AI infrastructure in India, including a $US110 billion ($156 billion) plan announced by Reliance ‌Industries on Thursday.

India's Tata Group also signed a partnership deal with OpenAI.

Mr Gates' cancellation follows the release of emails last month by the US Department of Justice that included communication between the late financier and convicted sex offender Epstein and the Gates Foundation's staff.

Millions more 'Epstein files' released by US government The US Department of Justice says it is releasing more than 3 million new documents from the "Epstein files".

The foundation said the billionaire will not deliver his address "to ensure the focus remains on the AI Summit's key priorities".

Only days ago the foundation had dismissed rumours of his ‌absence and insisted he was on track to attend.

The foundation's chief strategy officer and Africa and India chief Ankur Vora spoke instead of Mr Gates.

A representative for the philanthropic organisation, founded by Mr Gates and his then-wife in 2000, did not respond to a Reuters query on whether the withdrawal was linked to scrutiny over the Epstein files.

Mr Gates has said the relationship was confined to philanthropy-related discussions and that it was a mistake ‌for him to meet the sex offender.

Nvidia head Jensen Huang also pulled out of the AI Impact Summit. (Reuters: Steve Marcus, file)

He was among the top tech leaders scheduled to attend the event alongside Google CEO Sundar Pichai, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, and Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei.

Mr Gates' absence followed another high-profile cancellation by Nvidia's Jensen Huang earlier on Saturday, which added to a difficult opening for a summit billed as the first major AI forum in the Global South where India has sought to position itself as a leading voice in worldwide AI governance.

Modi address, AI commitments

In his keynote address Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi called for ensuring children's safety on AI platforms, speaking alongside French President Emmanuel Macron on Thursday.

Opposition parties argue Narendra Modi's government has poorly managed the ‌global summit. (AP: Indian Prime Minister's Office)

"We must be even more vigilant about children's safety," Mr Modi said.

" Just as a school syllabus is curated, the AI space should also be child and family-guided. "

Earlier, Mr Modi stood on stage with top AI executives and posed for ⁠photographs with their arms raised in a show of strength.

The photo shoot produced an awkward moment when Mr Altman and Mr Amodei, the chiefs of rival AI firms OpenAI and Anthropic, stood side by side on stage but did not hold hands, even though the other executives did.

Loading...

The symbolic unity pose was to mark the formal launch of the New Delhi Frontier AI Commitments, a set of voluntary principles adopted by leading AI companies at the summit to advance the inclusive, responsible development of frontier AI models.

"One hundred million people in India now use ChatGPT each week," Mr Altman told the gathering.

Chaos and traffic snarls

Despite the investment successes, India's first major AI summit has been marred by organisational lapses that have ‌left attendees shocked and angry over what they described as a ⁠lack of planning by the Indian government.

The summit exhibition halls were shut to the public on Thursday in a surprise move that sparked anger among participating companies that had set up stalls and pavilions.

The venue compound was largely deserted after three days of large crowds at the event.

A traffic jam near Bharat Mandapam, one of the venues of the AI Impact Summit in New Delhi. (Reuters: Bhawika Chhabra)

Indian university Galgotias was asked to vacate its stall after a staff member presented a commercially available robotic dog made in China as its own creation, sparking a public uproar.

Police ⁠repeatedly shut roads to give preference to VIP movement at the summit, creating chaos in the city of 20 million people.

The Indian government has apologised for the inconvenience caused to attendees in the initial days.

Opposition parties attacked the government and ‌the prime minister for poorly managing the ‌global summit.

"The whole summit is, sorry, was, meant for researchers, founders, builders who are grinding in the field every day," Microsoft researcher Jay Gala said on X.

"Instead we get treated like we don't matter, blocked for hours so some minister or official can pass through."

Reuters

title: This regional city is looking to a modular solution for the housing crisis
text:
The Housing Industry of Australia is warning shortages in regional Australia will get worse as costs rise and workforce shortages continue.

The shortage is already being felt in the north-west of WA, where local trades are almost impossible to access and the lack of housing stops people coming in for the work.

The Pilbara city of Karratha, 1,530 kilometres north of Perth, is hoping modular housing will be a solution.

Tradies are in high demand across Australia. (ABC News: Clare Sibthorpe)

Staff shortages delay projects

The remote mining city is in the midst of the boom part of the region's notorious regular boom and bust cycle.

Local building company TIMIK executive director Tim Fleisher said local staffing shortages was an ongoing struggle, with the company having to find trades from out of town.

Tim Fleischer says finding accommodation for staff is a major challenge. (ABC News: Kimberley Putland)

"Now that the housing market's up and running again, after coming into this boom, it's hard to get qualified guys," Mr Fleischer said.

"Our roof framing and wall framing guys are coming from Broome, so a thousand kilometres away, and our roof sheeting teams coming from Perth, so those guys fly up and back."

Accommodation is also a major challenge for Mr Fleischer.

Rising land prices blocking new housing, report finds A Housing Industry Association report says rising land prices are blocking the construction of new homes.

"You can find carpenters sometimes and they'll come through and go, 'Have you got accommodation? No? OK, I'll look somewhere else,'" he said.

" The fact of the matter is, unless you're a mining company, you can't just afford to have guys in a camp at $400 a night. "

Mr Fleischer said delays in one part of the building process could have knock-on effects that could mean builds were delayed by months.

An overlooked housing solution The public policy think tank, CEDA, believes Australia could increase its current housing stock by nine per cent if just one in four standalone homes in the five biggest cities are developed into dual occupancies.

Executive director of HIA Western Australia, Michael McGowan, said these challenges could hinder builders' capacity to take on new projects.

"It's incredibly risky for them to look to meet the demand of regional WA when they're not sure the skills are going to be there to support the construction," Mr McGowan said.

Michael McGowan says it is more feasible to develop apprentices in Pilbara. ( ABC News: Tabarak Al Jrood )

Mr McGowan said prior to COVID, the Pilbara housing construction market had been "really subdued", but since the pandemic, the workforce had not kept pace with the demand for housing.

"We can develop apprentices that are in the area and create some great opportunities," he said.

" But as far as attracting trades from around the rest of Australia, where maybe it's not as busy as it is in the Pilbara, it's challenging because we don't have too many places for them to live. "

Modular solution to rent

Locals Ian Abbott and Carissa Ives created a company two years ago, with its main focus on small developments.

The company is currently developing 12 build-to-rent properties in Karratha, utilising modular housing fabricated in Perth.

Modular housing is prefabricated in a factory, complete with bathrooms, a kitchen and internal fittings, before being transported to site and assembled on prepared foundations or footings.

It is considered to be more cost-effective than traditional builds due to reduced labour costs through off-site manufacturing.

Project manager Carissa Ives said modular housing made sense in the current development environment for Karratha.

Modular housing may the answer to shortages in Karratha. (ABC News: Kimberley Putland)

"We looked at all of our options and time frames if we would have them built locally or if we were to have them brought in modular," Ms Ives said.

"For us, it was more of a time and cost-effective way to have them build modular in WA and then brought to Karratha.

"We knew that obviously the availability of builders was quite limited."

Builders say there is a labour shortage in Karratha, and they have to manage workloads accordingly. (ABC News: Kimberley Putland)

Ms Ives said timing was also a factor, with land purchased through Development WA subject to time limits.

She said each modular house had a four-week turnaround time, including manufacturing, assembly and fit-out, with 12 homes expected to be completed within eight months.

The City of Karratha has also looked to modular as a quick solution, with its Project Dorothy apartment developments set to be installed later this year.

The project will see three modular apartment complexes installed in the suburb of Bulgarra, supplying more than 100 new rental properties.

title: Grieving dad plans surf lifesaving gala after shark attack tragedy
text:
The ocean off south-east Queensland's Bribie Island is where father Steven Zmuda feels closest to his late daughter.

"That first swim after she passed … I grabbed the water and just pushed it up to my face and I could feel Charlize's presence with that," he said.

Charlize Zmuda was swimming at Woorim Beach, Bribie Island, last year when she was attacked by a shark.

Charlize Zmuda, 17, was swimming at Woorim Beach, Bribie Island, last year when she was attacked by a shark. (Supplied: Zmuda family)

Emergency services were called to the unpatrolled beach just before 5pm on February 3, but the 17-year-old could not be saved.

Reflecting on the last year without Charlize, Mr Zmuda sighed heavily.

"As good as I can be for what it's like when you lose a child," he said.

Mr Zmuda, a proud member of the Bribie Island Surf Life Saving Club, often visits Fifth Avenue at Woorim Beach.

Steven Zmuda misses Charlize every day. (Supplied: Zmuda family)

His wife and Charlize's mother Renee Zmuda, is there every sunrise, drawing a heart in the sand and writing a message for her eldest daughter.

It's where they feel Charlize's presence.

"It's the most important spot because that's where Charlize rested at when she came in off the beach," Mr Zmuda said.

Friends and family gathered at a Bribie Island beach for the one-year anniversary of Charlize's death. (Supplied: Zmuda family)

The Moreton Bay Council announced on October 15, 2025 — what would have been the teenager's 18th birthday — that the area will be named 'Charlize's Place', with a plaque yet to be erected.

Councillor Brooke Savige said she had never known a 17-year-old who had touched so many others in their lifetime.

"The ocean is cleansing and I think that's a really beautiful way to remember Charlize and provide a nice, quiet place of reflection," she said.

Ocean-loving, hardworking, musically gifted

The ocean had always been central to Charlize's character, her dad explained.

"The dolphins, the turtles, everything out there in the ocean she loved," Mr Zmuda said.

"She'd spend so much time at the beach."

Charlize started as a Bribie Island Surf Life Saving Club nipper from the age of eight, was vice-captain of her patrol, and competed with the IRB team in the Lifesaving World Championships.

"She was about to go into the process of getting a silver medallion so that she could become a patrol captain at the beach," Mr Zmuda said.

Charlize Zmuda starred in many high school musicals. (Supplied: Zmuda family)

"We were about to buy her a boat driver's ticket so she could start driving the IRBs [inflatable rescue boats] on the beach as well."

Mr Zmuda said his daughter was cheeky, fun loving, kind, musically gifted, caring, and a hard worker.

She could play almost anything on the piano by ear and starred in numerous school musicals.

"She'd always be on the piano behind me playing while I'm trying to watch the television," Mr Zmuda said.

" I miss it so much. "

Charlize worked at a local takeaway shop and always helped with chores at home.

"Up on the board at home, we've got a to-do list for the week, and it's still got the shifts that she was meant to do," Mr Zmuda said.

Four days before her death Charlize attended her school formal. She had her first kiss at the after party.

A huge crowd gathered on Woorim Beach on February 4, 2025, the day after Charlize Zmuda died in a shark attack. (ABC News: Will Murray)

"She was out with her friends most nights enjoying life as a teenager does," her father said.

"You don't realise at that time how precious the moments are because you don't realise, they're going to be taken away from you."

Charlize's legacy lives on

The Zmuda family is working with Ariana Doolan, Member for Pumicestone, and Surf Life Saving Queensland to plan a yearly gala in Charlize's name.

It will fundraise money for surf lifesaving clubs across the state.

"It's going to be special because she was a special girl and her legacy, even though she's gone, is never going to be forgotten," Mr Zmuda said.

Charlize Zmuda (left) loved the beach. (Supplied)

The first gala is set to be held in November this year.

"The vision is to turn grief into something that helps others … her legacy is one of care and service," Ms Doolan said.

"Charlize was passionate about the ocean, and she would have wanted more people to be part of surf lifesaving."

Surf Life Saving Queensland has been contacted for comment.

Charlize Zmuda took school formal pictures at the beach just days before her death. (Supplied)

'Don't live in fear'

Mr Zmuda said he did not want his daughter's death to stop anyone from enjoying Queensland's shorelines.

"Be aware of what's out there, but don't be fearful of that, because you've got a bigger chance of being struck by lightning, three times a bigger chance than what happened to Charlize," he said.

Charlize Zmuda started as a Bribie Island nipper from the age of eight. (Supplied)

When the beach reopened days after Charlize's death, Mr Zmuda and his youngest daughter Stephanie, were the first to step into the water.

"To show people not to be scared," he said.

"She wants us all, including our family, to live our lives, and enjoy our lives."

title: Twelve or 16? Extent of age limit for e-bike riders under scrutiny
text:
Advertisement

NationalNSWCycling Twelve or 16? Extent of age limit for e-bike riders under scrutiny Matt O'Sullivan February 20, 2026 — 1:19pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Save this article for later Add articles to your saved list and come back to them anytime. Got it Share A A A

E-bike riders will have to be at least 12 years old to use them, as part of a bid to crack down on unruly behaviour, although existing owners of high-powered bikes will have a three-year grace period before tougher standards are enforced. The NSW government is considering raising the age limit for e-bike riders to as old as 16, although Transport Minister John Graham said he wanted time to decide exactly what it should be, and “whether it should be 12 or 16”. Transport Minister John Graham, centre, unveils his latest plans on Friday to address concerns about e-bikes. Steven Siewert “It’s not a simple call, and it’s one that we’ve got to make, not just for Sydney, but for all of NSW,” he said, adding that a higher age limit might be a concern in regional areas where teenagers used e-bikes to get to school. A review led by Transport for NSW will recommend a legal minimum age between 12 and 16 for riding an e-bike in the state, while considering whether children and teenagers have the skills, maturity and awareness of potential dangers required to safely carry passengers.

Advertisement

Advice and findings will be provided to Graham and Roads Minister Jenny Aitchison by June, and the government will make a final decision on an age limit and passengers. “At the moment, it’s legal for a primary school kid with no peripheral vision who can barely lift one of these bikes to ride it on NSW streets. We want that to change,” Graham said. “It’s simply dangerous to have kids as young as 10 or 11 on these high-powered bikes.” E-bikes have grown in popularity in Sydney in recent years. Steven Siewert The government will also adopt a European safety and performance standard – known as EN15194 – to ensure e-bikes are like bicycles, and that high-powered, illegal motorbikes masquerading as e-bikes are pulled from roads and footpaths. However, a three-year transition period will be applied given that many e-bikes were purchased legally under a rule change several years ago which increased the allowable power from 250 watts to 500 watts. While NSW will adopt the European standard next month, it will not be enforced on e-bikes that fail to meet it until March 2029.

Advertisement

Bicycle NSW chief executive Peter McLean said it was the “gold standard” for e-bikes across the world, making them safe and high quality. “It’s safe for the riders, and it is safe for all road users, including, of course, pedestrians,” he said. Related Article Updated
